summary: Fowler shares an insider look at Thoughtworks in early 2005, a company he joined because he found it special and unusual. The firm had grown 50% to $75 million in revenue and nearly 600 employees, though growth exceeded his comfort level. He highlights their rigorous hiring standards targeting the top 0.5-1% of developers, preferring false negatives over bad hires. The post emphasizes their commitment to agile principles, integrated testing practices, and the goal of becoming a fully employee-owned company. Fowler acknowledges ongoing challenges with project selection and the need to ensure work remains fulfilling beyond just profitable.
key_points: Thoughtworks maintains extremely selective hiring (top 0.5-1% of developers), accepting false negatives over hiring mistakes
The company integrates testing throughout development at multiple levels (unit, acceptance) and across roles (programmers, analysts)
Growth of 50% annually, while successful, exceeds Fowler's comfort level
A key organizational goal is becoming fully employee-owned and controlled
Project selection remains a challenge - profitable work isn't always fulfilling work
money_quotes: The reason I gave up my successful life as an independent consultant was because I felt Thoughtworks was a special and unusual company.
I'm sure the recruiting system produces too many false negatives (people we turn down who we should have hired), but I'd still rather take that than the alternative (hiring people we shouldn't).
We tend to hire people who really enjoy delivering applications that matter - and still too many projects throw up all sorts of frustrations that get in the way.
Part of the point of Thoughtworks is to do enjoyable and fulfilling work - money isn't the primary reason most of us are here.
There is no such thing as Thoughtworks's corporate opinion, what we have is the opinion of our employees.
It's a simple slogan, but not easy to sort out the mechanics for a company of our size and international scope.
key_insight: A technology consultancy's long-term success depends not just on hiring elite talent but on selecting projects that keep that talent engaged and fulfilled.
